title: Coin Toss Game (HT)

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: Players A and B alternate flipping a fair coin (A goes first). The game ends as soon as the sequence H followed by T appears anywhere in the toss history. Whichever player tossed the T wins.
rules: Fair coin; players alternate (A flips, then B flips, then A, ...).
Game ends when the most recent two flips are H (penultimate) then T (last).
The player who tossed the LAST flip (the T) wins.
setup.question: What is P(A wins)?

learningObjectives: Define P(A) and condition on A's first toss to set up an equation.
Solve a sub-equation P(A | H) = 1/3 (where A's first toss was H).

stages.label: Condition on A's first toss
stages.prompt: Let P(A) = probability A wins. Condition on A's first toss: P(A) = (1/2)*P(A|T) + (1/2)*P(A|H). What is P(A | T)?

options.text: P(A | T) = P(B), because after A throws T, B is in the role of 'first to toss'.  By symmetry of the rule, P(A | T) = 1 - P(A).

stages.explanation: After A's first toss is T, no HT yet (no H exists). The game is now from B's perspective with B as the 'lead'. So P(A | T) = P(B from this point) = 1 - P(A).

stages.label: Set up P(A | H)
stages.prompt: If A's first toss is H, the next toss (by B) decides. If B tosses T, the H-T pattern completes and B wins -- A loses. If B tosses H, B has just tossed an H. A's situation is now... ?

options.text: A is now in the position 'last toss was H, my turn to toss'. By symmetry of the after-H game, P(A wins from here) = 1 - P(A|H).

stages.explanation: After B tosses H, the game continues with H 'in play'. A is now in a sub-game where A's task is what B faced before -- exact role swap. So P(A wins from here) = 1 - P(A | H).

stages.label: Commit

options.text: P(A) = 4/9 ≈ 0.444.  P(B) = 5/9.  B has the advantage.

stages.explanation: P(A) = 4/9. The first player has a DISADVANTAGE because A cannot win on the first toss (no H yet to follow with T). B can win on the second toss (if A threw H, B throws T).

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Verify by simulation or careful enumeration of the first few moves.
What if the winning pattern is HH (instead of HT)? Compute P(A wins).
Why does HT favor B but HH favor A?
